body.label: source_id.root.theorem
body.claim: Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space. Let $x$ and $y$ be two distinct points with distance $d(x, y) = \epsilon > 0$. Then the open balls $B(x, \epsilon/3)$ and $B(y, \epsilon/3)$ do not intersect.

proof.assumption: \(B(x,\varepsilon/3)\cap B(y,\varepsilon/3)\neq\varnothing\), equivalently there exists \(z\in X\) lying in both balls.

proof_steps.claim_label: Set up the negation of the desired disjointness claim.
Obtain a point in the intersection.
Obtain a witness point in the intersection.

proof_steps.claim: Verify that the witness lies in the required intersection.
proof_steps.text: The chosen point satisfies \(z\in B(x,\varepsilon/3)\cap B(y,\varepsilon/3)\).

proof_steps.claim: Verify the witness lies in both balls.
proof_steps.text: From \(z\in B(x,\varepsilon/3)\cap B(y,\varepsilon/3)\), conclude \(z\in B(x,\varepsilon/3)\) and \(z\in B(y,\varepsilon/3)\).

proof_steps.claim: Extract the metric distance bounds from the ball memberships.
proof_steps.text: From \(z\in B(x,\varepsilon/3)\) and \(z\in B(y,\varepsilon/3)\), obtain \(d(z,x)<\varepsilon/3\) and \(d(z,y)<\varepsilon/3\). Since \(d\) is symmetric, this also gives \(d(x,z)<\varepsilon/3\).

calculation_sequence: d(x,y) < 2\varepsilon/3 (By the triangle inequality, d(x,y) \le d(x,z)+d(z,y), and using d(x,z)<\varepsilon/3 and d(z,y)<\varepsilon/3 gives d(x,z)+d(z,y)<\varepsilon/3+\varepsilon/3=2\varepsilon/3.)
